113: - System Learning Loop
114:   - See: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go/.kb/models/system-learning-loop.md
115:   - Summary:
116:     The System Learning Loop is the third layer of the Pressure Visibility System that automatically converts recurring context gaps into actionable improvements. It tracks gaps during spawns, identifies patterns using RecurrenceThreshold=3, and suggests specific actions (kn entries, beads issues, investigations). The system uses shell-aware command parsing to generate runnable commands with proper quoted string handling, and ensures minimum length requirements for downstream tools (kn requires 20+ chars). This creates a closed feedback loop: gaps → patterns → suggestions → improvements → fewer gaps.
117:     
118:     ---
119:   - Critical Invariants:
120:     1. **RecurrenceThreshold = 3** - Pattern detection balances noise (1) vs signal (3+)
121:     2. **All matching events must be marked resolved** - Not just the most recent one
122:     3. **FindRecurringGaps excludes resolved events** - Prevents resolved gaps from reappearing
123:     4. **Shell-aware command parsing required** - Quoted strings with spaces must be preserved
124:     5. **Minimum 20-character reasons** - kn decide/constrain requirement enforced at generation time
125:     6. **30-day retention window** - Gap events older than 30 days are pruned
126:     7. **Gap recording happens after gating** - Captures all gaps whether spawn proceeds or not
127:     
128:     ---
129:   - Why This Fails:
130:     ### Failure Mode 1: Resolved Gaps Keep Appearing
131:     
132:     **Symptom:** `orch learn` shows same gap after running `orch learn act` to resolve it
133:     
134:     **Root cause:** Two bugs combined:
135:     1. `RecordResolution` only marked the most recent event (used `break` after first match)
136:     2. `FindRecurringGaps` counted all events without filtering by Resolution field
137:     
138:     **Why it happens:**
139:     - Gap occurs 5 times → user resolves → only 1 event marked
140:     - Next `FindRecurringGaps` call counts 4 unresolved events → still above threshold (3)
141:     - Same pattern keeps appearing despite resolution
142:     
143:     **Impact:**
144:     - User frustration ("I already fixed this!")
145:     - Loss of trust in learning system
146:     - Duplicate work
147:     
148:     **Fix:**
149:     - `RecordResolution` now marks ALL matching events (removed `break`)
150:     - `FindRecurringGaps` filters out resolved events before counting
151:     
152:     **Source:** `.kb/investigations/2025-12-25-inv-orch-learn-resolved-gaps-still.md`
153:     
154:     ---
155:     
156:     ### Failure Mode 2: Generated Commands Fail Due to Broken Quoting
157:     
158:     **Symptom:** `orch learn act N` generates command that fails when executed
159:     
160:     **Root cause:** Using `strings.Fields()` to parse commands - splits on whitespace without respecting quotes
161:     
162:     **Why it happens:**
163:     - Command: `kn decide "auth" --reason "Used by: investigation. Occurred 5 times"`
164:     - `strings.Fields` splits into: `["kn", "decide", "\"auth\"", "--reason", "\"Used", "by:", "investigation.", ...]`
165:     - Shell receives mangled arguments, command fails
166:     
167:     **Impact:**
168:     - Learning loop broken - suggestions can't be executed
169:     - User must manually reconstruct commands
170:     - Reduces value of automated suggestions
171:     
172:     **Fix:**
173:     - Added `ParseShellCommand()` with shell-aware quote handling
174:     - Respects double and single quotes as argument delimiters
175:     - Added `ValidateCommand()` to catch malformed commands before execution
176:     
177:     **Source:** `.kb/investigations/2025-12-26-inv-orch-learn-act-commands-should.md`
178:     
179:     ---
180:     
181:     ### Failure Mode 3: Short Reasons Fail kn Validation
182:     
183:     **Symptom:** `orch learn act` generates kn command that fails with "reason too short" error
184:     
185:     **Root cause:** `generateReasonFromGaps` produced "Occurred N times" (16 chars) when gap events lacked skill/task metadata
186:     
187:     **Why it happens:**
188:     - Gap events with sparse metadata → only occurrence count available
189:     - "Occurred 3 times" = 16 characters
190:     - kn requires 20+ characters for `--reason` flag
191:     - Command fails validation at execution time
192:     
193:     **Impact:**
194:     - Generated commands unusable
195:     - User must manually edit reason strings
196:     - Learning loop broken for sparse gaps

202: - Agent Lifecycle State Model
203:   - See: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go/.kb/models/agent-lifecycle-state-model.md
204:   - Summary:
205:     Agent state exists across **four independent layers** (tmux windows, OpenCode in-memory, OpenCode on-disk, beads comments). These layers fall into two distinct categories: **state layers** (beads, workspace files) that represent what work was done, and **infrastructure layers** (OpenCode sessions, tmux windows) that represent transient execution resources. The dashboard reconciles these via a **Priority Cascade**: check beads issue status first (highest authority), then Phase comments, then registry state, then session existence. Status can appear "wrong" at the dashboard level while being "correct" at each individual layer - this is a measurement artifact from combining multiple sources of truth.
206:     
207:     ---
208:   - Critical Invariants:
209:     1. **Phase: Complete is agent's declaration** - Only agent can reach this, not orchestrator
210:     2. **Beads issue closed = canonical completion** - All status queries defer to beads
211:     3. **Session existence ≠ agent still working** - Sessions persist indefinitely
212:     4. **Status checks don't mutate state** - Calculation is read-only, no side effects
213:     5. **Multiple sources must be reconciled** - No single source has complete truth
214:     6. **Tmux windows are UI layer only** - Not authoritative for state
215:     
216:     ---
217:   - Why This Fails:
218:     ### Failure Mode 1: Dashboard Shows "Active" When Agent is Done
219:     
220:     **Symptom:** Dashboard shows agent as active, but `bd show <id>` says status=closed
221:     
222:     **Root cause:** Dashboard caching or SSE lag - hasn't received beads update yet
223:     
224:     **Why it happens:**
225:     - Agent reaches Phase: Complete
226:     - `orch complete` closes beads issue
227:     - Beads issue status = closed
228:     - Dashboard hasn't refreshed or polled beads yet
229:     - Dashboard still shows cached "active" state
230:     
231:     **Fix:** Refresh dashboard browser tab (forces beads query)
232:     
233:     **NOT the fix:** Deleting OpenCode session (treats symptom, not cause)
234:     
235:     ### Failure Mode 2: "Dead" Agents That Actually Completed
236:     
237:     **Symptom:** Dashboard shows "dead", but work is done and beads issue closed
238:     
239:     **Root cause:** Session cleanup happened before dashboard queried, cascade reached session check
240:     
241:     **Why it happens:**
242:     - Agent completed, beads issue closed
243:     - Session cleanup ran (manual or automatic)
244:     - Dashboard cascade: beads check → no issue (closed) → session check → no session → "dead"
245:     
246:     **Fix (Jan 8):** Priority Cascade puts beads/Phase check before session existence check
247:     
248:     ### Failure Mode 3: Agent Went Idle But Not Complete
249:     
250:     **Symptom:** Session status is "idle" but no `Phase: Complete` comment
251:     
252:     **Root cause:** Agent ran out of context, crashed, or didn't follow completion protocol
253:     
254:     **Why it happens:**
255:     - Session exhausts context (150k tokens)
256:     - Agent stops responding
257:     - SSE event: `session.status = idle`
258:     - No `Phase: Complete` was ever written
259:     - Dashboard shows "idle" or "waiting"
260:     
261:     **This is expected behavior.** Session idle ≠ work complete. Only agents that explicitly run `bd comment <id> "Phase: Complete"` are considered done.
262:     
263:     **Fix:** Check workspace for what agent accomplished, then either:
264:     - `orch complete <id> --force` if work is done
265:     - `orch abandon <id>` if work is incomplete
266:     
267:     ### Failure Mode 4: Cross-Project Agents Not Visible
268:     
269:     **Symptom:** Agent spawned with `--workdir /other/project` doesn't appear in dashboard
270:     
271:     **Root cause:** Dashboard only scans current project's `.orch/workspace/` directory
272:     
273:     **Why it happens:**
274:     - Workspace created in `/other/project/.orch/workspace/`
275:     - Dashboard running from `orch-go` only sees `orch-go/.orch/workspace/`
276:     - Cross-project discovery requires querying OpenCode sessions for unique directories
277:     
278:     **Fix (Jan 6):** Multi-project workspace cache built from OpenCode session directories
279:     
280:     ---
281:   - Your findings should confirm, contradict, or extend the claims above.

285: - Dashboard Architecture
286:   - See: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go/.kb/models/dashboard-architecture.md
287:   - Summary:
288:     The Swarm Dashboard is a Svelte 5 web UI served by `orch serve` (Go backend) that provides real-time monitoring of agent status, daemon health, and operational metrics.
289:     
290:     **Critical context (Option A+):** The dashboard is Dylan's (meta-orchestrator's) ONLY observability layer. He does not use CLI tools directly. Dashboard failure = Dylan is blind. This makes dashboard reliability tier-0 infrastructure. See orchestrator skill "Observability Architecture (Option A+)" section.
291:     
292:     The architecture uses a **two-mode design** (Operational/Historical) to separate daily coordination from deep analysis. SSE connections enable real-time updates but are constrained by HTTP/1.1's 6-connection limit. Progressive disclosure and stable sorting prevent information overload while maintaining scan-ability.
293:     
294:     ---
295:   - Critical Invariants:
296:     1. **Two-mode design is mutually exclusive** - Cannot show both Operational and Historical views simultaneously
297:     2. **SSE Events auto-connect, Agentlog is opt-in** - Connection pool management
298:     3. **beadsFetchThreshold controls remote queries** - 5+ ready issues triggers `bd ready` shell-out
299:     4. **Progressive disclosure via collapsed panels** - Event panels start collapsed, expand on click
300:     5. **Stable sort maintains scan-ability** - Agent order doesn't change unless status changes
301:     6. **Early filtering reduces payload size** - Backend filters before sending to frontend
302:     
303:     ---
304:   - Why This Fails:
305:     ### Failure Mode 1: Connection Pool Exhaustion
306:     
307:     **Symptom:** API fetches hang or timeout when SSE panels open
308:     
309:     **Root cause:** HTTP/1.1 allows only 6 connections per origin; SSE occupies slots
310:     
311:     **Why it happens:**
312:     - Events SSE (auto-connect): 1 slot
313:     - Agentlog SSE (auto-connect before fix): 1 slot
314:     - Remaining 4 slots for API fetches
315:     - If 5+ API requests concurrent, some block
316:     
317:     **Fix (Jan 5):** Made Agentlog SSE opt-in via Follow button, freeing 1 slot
318:     
319:     ### Failure Mode 2: Slow Dashboard Load with 100+ Agents
320:     
321:     **Symptom:** Dashboard takes 5-10 seconds to load with many agents
322:     
323:     **Root cause:** `/api/agents` endpoint performs expensive operations (OpenCode queries, beads parsing) synchronously
324:     
325:     **Why it happens:**
326:     - Each agent requires OpenCode session query
327:     - Full beads issue parsing for each agent
328:     - No caching, recomputed on every request
329:     
330:     **Fix (Jan 6):** Response caching with 2-second TTL, reduced load time to <1 second
331:     
332:     ### Failure Mode 3: Information Overload in Operational Mode
333:     
334:     **Symptom:** Users overwhelmed by full swarm map with 50+ agents
335:     
336:     **Root cause:** Single view tried to serve both daily coordination and deep analysis
337:     
338:     **Why it happens:**
339:     - Operational needs: "What's ready? What's broken?"
340:     - Historical needs: "Show me everything, all filters, full archive"
341:     - One view can't optimize for both
342:     
343:     **Fix (Jan 7):** Two-mode design - Operational (focused) vs Historical (comprehensive)
344:     
345:     ### Failure Mode 4: Plugin Cascade (Dashboard "Disconnected" Despite Services Running)
346:     
347:     **Symptom:** Dashboard shows "disconnected", `overmind status` shows all 3 services running, but `orch status` returns HTTP 500
348:     
349:     **Root cause:** OpenCode plugin error (e.g., v1→v2 API incompatibility) crashes OpenCode's internal request handling
350:     
351:     **Why it happens:**
352:     - OpenCode loads plugins at startup
353:     - Bad plugin throws error on every request
354:     - `/api/agents` calls OpenCode → gets 500
355:     - Dashboard can't fetch agent data → shows "disconnected"
356:     - overmind sees process running (not crashed) → reports "running"
357:     
358:     **Cascade:**
359:     ```
360:     Plugin error → OpenCode internal 500 → orch status fails → API can't get agents → Dashboard "disconnected"
361:     ```
362:     
363:     **Fix (Jan 14):** Disable plugins, restart OpenCode, re-enable one-by-one. Root cause was session-resume.js using v1 API (object export) instead of v2 (function export).
364:     
365:     **Key insight:** Dashboard can appear "down" while all processes are technically "running". Health checks must verify data flow, not just port availability.
366:     
367:     ---

369: - Model Access and Spawn Paths
370:   - See: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go/.kb/models/model-access-spawn-paths.md
371:   - Summary:
372:     Anthropic restricts Opus 4.5 access via fingerprinting that blocks API usage but allows Claude Code CLI with Max subscription. This constraint forced a **dual spawn architecture**: primary path (OpenCode API + Sonnet/Flash, headless, high concurrency) and escape hatch (Claude CLI + Opus, tmux, crash-resistant). The escape hatch exists because critical infrastructure work (fixing the spawn system itself) can't depend on what might fail. Model choice now encodes reliability requirements, not just quality preferences.
373:     
374:     ---
375:   - Critical Invariants:
376:     1. **Never spawn OpenCode infrastructure work without --backend claude --tmux**
377:        - Violation: Agent kills itself mid-execution when server restarts
378:     
379:     2. **Infrastructure detection runs before model auto-selection**
380:        - Priority 2.5 (between explicit flags and model-based selection)
381:        - Ensures auto-apply happens even without explicit --backend
382:     
383:     3. **Opus only accessible via Claude CLI backend**
384:        - API requests to Opus fail with auth error
385:        - Fingerprinting checks more than headers (TLS, HTTP/2 frames, ordering)
386:     
387:     4. **Escape hatch provides true independence**
388:        - Claude CLI binary ≠ OpenCode server
389:        - Tmux session persists across service restarts
390:        - Different authentication path (Max subscription OAuth)
391:     
392:     ---
393:   - Why This Fails:
394:     ### Failure Mode 1: Zombie Agents (Jan 8, 2026)
395:     
396:     **Symptom:** Agents tracked in registry but never actually ran
397:     
398:     **Root Cause:** Spawning with `--model opus` before understanding auth gate
399:     - orch created registry entry
400:     - OpenCode session created
401:     - Anthropic rejected API request (fingerprinting)
402:     - Agent hung in "running" state
403:     - Consumed concurrency slot without doing work
404:     
405:     **Examples:**
406:     - orch-go-mo0ja, orch-go-pzi2i, orch-go-aoei0, orch-go-gd1gd, orch-go-lwc3o
407:     
408:     **Fix:** Never use `--model opus` without `--backend claude`
409:     
410:     ### Failure Mode 2: Header Injection Conflicts (Jan 8, 2026)
411:     
412:     **Symptom:** Gemini Flash spawns hung after attempting Opus bypass
413:     
414:     **Root Cause:** Injected Claude Code headers (`x-app: cli`, `anthropic-version`, etc.) into OpenCode's Anthropic provider
415:     - Bypassed Opus gate (didn't work)
416:     - Broke Gemini spawns (headers conflicted with Bun fetch/SDK)
417:     - System-wide impact from localized change
418:     
419:     **Lesson:** Fingerprinting is more sophisticated than headers alone
420:     
421:     ### Failure Mode 3: Infrastructure Work Kills Itself
422:     
423:     **Symptom:** Agent fixing OpenCode server crashes mid-execution
424:     
425:     **Root Cause:** Agent spawned via OpenCode API, agent's fix restarts OpenCode server, agent's session killed

429:     **Why auto-detection matters:**

437: - Session Deletion Vectors
438:   - See: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go/.kb/models/session-deletion-vectors.md
439:   - Summary:
440:     Active OpenCode sessions can become unfindable through **7 independent vectors** spanning 3 systems (disk-cleanup.sh, orch-go cleanup, OpenCode itself). The fundamental problem is that no "session is active, do not touch" lock exists, and multiple processes can delete sessions from the shared SQLite database without coordination. The Ctrl+D keybind is triple-bound (app exit, session delete, input delete), creating the highest-risk accidental deletion path. The disk-cleanup.sh vector was the first confirmed root cause (now fixed), but the bug persists because at least two other vectors remain open.
441:     
442:     ---
443:   - Critical Invariants:
444:     1. **Sessions exist in SQLite or they don't** - There is no "evicted but recoverable" state
445:     2. **NotFoundError = row deleted from DB** - Not a caching issue
446:     3. **Multiple processes share one SQLite DB** - No coordination protocol
447:     4. **Cascade deletes propagate silently** - Deleting a session kills all messages and parts
448:     5. **No "active session" lock exists** - Any process can delete any session at any time
449:     6. **JSON→SQLite migration is one-time** - Gate checks `opencode.db` existence, not whether sessions were imported. DB existed from Jan 27 schema migrations → 188 JSON sessions permanently orphaned, invisible to current code
450:     
451:     ---
452:   - Why This Fails:
453:     ### Failure Mode 1: Untracked Session Deletion (Vector #2)
454:     
455:     **Symptom:** Interactive TUI session disappears mid-conversation with NotFoundError
456:     
457:     **Root cause:** `cleanUntrackedDiskSessions()` in `clean_cmd.go:408-539` finds sessions not tracked by any `.orch/workspace/*/session_id` file, checks if they were updated in the last 5 minutes, and deletes them if idle.
458:     
459:     **Why TUI sessions are vulnerable:**
460:     - Interactive/orchestrator TUI sessions have NO workspace directory
461:     - No `.session_id` file means Layer 1 protection is bypassed entirely
462:     - If the user hasn't sent a message in >5 minutes (reading, thinking, context switch), Layer 2 (recency) marks it as orphaned
463:     - Layer 3 (`IsSessionProcessing()`) only runs for recently-active sessions
464:     - Session gets deleted via `client.DeleteSession(session.ID)` at line 525
465:     
466:     **Code path:**
467:     ```

513:     Beads integration uses **RPC-first with CLI fallback** pattern: try native Go RPC client (fast, no process spawn), fall back to CLI subprocess if daemon unavailable. The integration operates at **three points in agent lifecycle**: spawn (create issue), work (report phase via comments), complete (close with reason). **Auto-tracking** creates issues automatically unless `--no-track` flag set. The RPC client lives in **pkg/beads** (never shell out with `exec.Command` directly) and provides 10x performance improvement over CLI (single RPC call vs subprocess spawn + JSON parse).

515:     ---
516:   - Why This Fails:
517:     ### 1. RPC Client Unavailable
518:     
519:     **What happens:** RPC calls fail, client falls back to CLI, performance degrades.
520:     
521:     **Root cause:** Beads daemon not running. RPC socket `~/.beads/daemon.sock` doesn't exist.
522:     
523:     **Why detection is hard:** Fallback is silent. No warning that RPC failed. User sees slow performance, doesn't know why.
524:     
525:     **Fix:** Start beads daemon: `bd daemon start` or ensure launchd starts it on boot.
526:     
527:     **Detection:** Log RPC failures, surface in `orch doctor` health check.
528:     
529:     ### 2. Beads ID Not Found
530:     
531:     **What happens:** `orch complete orch-go-abc1` fails with "issue not found".
532:     
533:     **Root cause:** Cross-project spawn. Issue created in orch-knowledge, but trying to complete from orch-go. Beads scoped to current directory's `.beads/`.
534:     
535:     **Why detection is hard:** Beads ID looks valid (correct format), but doesn't exist in current project's `.beads/issues.jsonl`.
536:     
537:     **Fix:** `cd` into correct project before completion, or use `--workdir` flag.
538:     
539:     **Prevention:** `orch complete` should detect project from workspace, auto-cd.
540:     
541:     ### 3. Auto-Tracking Creates Duplicates
542:     
543:     **What happens:** `orch spawn` creates issue, but issue already exists for same work.
544:     
545:     **Root cause:** User creates issue manually, then spawns with auto-tracking. Both create issue.
546:     
547:     **Why detection is hard:** No deduplication. Beads doesn't check if similar issue exists.
548:     
549:     **Fix:** Use `--issue <id>` flag to reference existing issue instead of auto-creating.
550:     
551:     **Prevention:** Better UX: `orch spawn` could check for related issues and prompt user.

1404: ### Implementation Phase (Verification-First Mode)

1406: **Purpose:** Implement feature by specifying expected behavior, instrumenting verification, implementing, then verifying behavior matches spec.

1414: 4. **Verify:** Cross-reference behavior against spec + capture evidence per spec requirements
1415: 
1416: **Key difference from TDD:**

1418: - Verification-first: Read spec → write test that proves AC-xxx → implement → verify behavior matches spec

<content>1: # Design: Knowledge Lineage Tree Visualization
2: 
3: **Date:** 2026-02-14
4: **Status:** Proposed
5: **Owner:** Orchestrator System
6: **Context:** The knowledge base contains rich lineage relationships (investigations → decisions → issues, models → probes, guides ← investigations) but no way to visualize the natural parent-child structure. This design creates a tree-based visualization that serves as Dylan's primary interface for understanding project state.
7: 
8: ## Problem Statement
9: 
10: The knowledge base has grown to 861 investigations, 54 decisions, 41 models, and many guides, with natural parent-child relationships embedded in:
11: - Prior-Work tables in investigations
12: - Evidence sections in decisions  
13: - Synthesized From headers in models
14: - Filesystem hierarchy for probes
15: - Beads issue references to decisions
16: 
17: **Current pain:** No way to see the knowledge landscape, spot investigation clusters without decisions, understand where new work fits, or orient new session Claudes.
18: 
19: **The resonant example:** Dylan saw this tree rendering and said "wow, this is a really cool view":
20: 
21: ```
22: orch-go knowledge tree
23: │
24: ├─◉ Entropy Spiral
25: │ ├─◉ post-mortems/2026-01-02-system-spiral-dec27-jan02.md
26: │ ├─◉ investigations/2026-02-12-inv-entropy-spiral-postmortem.md
27: │ ├─◉ investigations/2026-02-14-inv-entropy-spiral-deep-analysis.md
28: │ │ ├─★ decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md
29: │ │ │ ├─● orch-go-5sc  Decision record                    CLOSED
30: │ │ │ ├─● orch-go-7jl  Daemon pause after N completions   CLOSED
31: │ │ │ └─◇ orch-go-6th  Skill system update                triage:review
32: │ │ └─● orch-go-agr  Trajectory audit                     IN PROGRESS
33: │ └─◉ handoffs/2026-02-13-entropy-spiral-recovery.md
34: ```
35: 
36: ## Design Questions
37: 
38: ### 1. Where Does This Render?
39: 
40: **Answer:** Both dashboard (persistent browsing) and orchestrator session (contextual verification).
41: 
42: **Dashboard (Primary):**
43: - **Location:** New route `/knowledge-tree` in web dashboard
44: - **Interaction:** Full-featured tree browser with expand/collapse, filtering, search
45: - **Persistence:** Tree state saved to localStorage (expanded nodes, filters)
46: - **Use cases:** Session start orientation, triage, exploration
47: 
48: **Orchestrator Session (Secondary):**
49: - **Location:** Inline rendering during `orch complete` verification flow
50: - **Interaction:** Read-only ASCII tree showing where new deliverable fits
51: - **Context:** Shown when daemon pauses after N completions, highlights new nodes
52: - **Use cases:** Verification context, explain-back preparation
53: 
54: **CLI Command (Tertiary):**
55: - **Command:** `orch tree` or `kb tree` for quick lookups
56: - **Output:** ASCII tree like example above
57: - **Options:** `--cluster <name>`, `--depth <n>`, `--format json|text`
58: - **Use cases:** Quick orientation, scripting, CI checks
59: 
60: **Recommendation:** Start with dashboard (primary value), add CLI command second (cheap), defer orchestrator integration (complex, needs verification flow design).
61: 
62: ### 2. Data Model: How to Extract the Tree?
63: 
64: **Answer:** Parse existing artifacts, no new metadata required. Relationships already exist.
65: 
66: **Extraction Sources:**
67: 
68: | Relationship | Source | Pattern | Example |
69: |--------------|--------|---------|---------|
70: | Investigation → Prior Work | `Prior-Work` table in investigation.md | Markdown table row | `\| .kb/investigations/...md \| extends \| yes \| None \|` |
71: | Decision → Evidence | `Evidence` section in decision.md | Inline references | Reference to investigation path |
72: | Model → Investigations | `Synthesized From:` header in model.md | Header value | `31 investigations + completion.md guide...` |
73: | Model → Probes | Filesystem hierarchy | `.kb/models/{name}/probes/*.md` | Directory structure |
74: | Decision → Issues | Context section in decision.md | Beads ID references | `Related Epic: orch-go-xxxxx` |
75: | Issue → Decision | Beads issue description | Description text | Reference to decision path |
76: | Guide → Investigations | Guide frontmatter/content | Inline references | References to investigation paths |
77: 
78: **Extraction Algorithm:**
79: 
80: ```go
81: type KnowledgeNode struct {
82:     ID           string                 // Unique identifier
83:     Type         string                 // investigation, decision, model, probe, guide, issue
84:     Path         string                 // File path or beads ID
85:     Title        string                 // Display name
86:     Children     []*KnowledgeNode       // Child nodes
87:     Metadata     map[string]interface{} // Status, dates, etc.
88: }
89: 
90: func ExtractKnowledgeTree(rootDir string) (*KnowledgeNode, error) {
91:     root := &KnowledgeNode{Type: "root", Title: "orch-go knowledge"}
92:     
93:     // 1. Parse all .kb/ files, extract metadata
94:     investigations := parseInvestigations(rootDir + "/.kb/investigations")
95:     decisions := parseDecisions(rootDir + "/.kb/decisions")
96:     models := parseModels(rootDir + "/.kb/models")
97:     guides := parseGuides(rootDir + "/.kb/guides")
98:     
99:     // 2. Build relationship graph from Prior-Work tables, Evidence sections, etc.
100:     graph := buildRelationshipGraph(investigations, decisions, models, guides)
101:     
102:     // 3. Cluster by topic (inferred from area labels, filesystem paths, or explicit tags)
103:     clusters := inferTopLevelClusters(graph)
104:     
105:     // 4. For each cluster, build tree by following parent→child edges
106:     for _, cluster := range clusters {
107:         clusterNode := buildClusterTree(cluster, graph)
108:         root.Children = append(root.Children, clusterNode)
109:     }
110:     
111:     return root, nil
112: }
113: ```
114: 
115: **Prior-Work Table Parser:**
116: ```go
117: func parsePriorWorkTable(markdown string) []Relationship {
118:     // Find "## Prior Work" section
119:     // Extract markdown table rows
120:     // Parse: | path | relationship | verified | conflicts |
121:     // Return structured relationships
122: }
123: ```
124: 
125: **No Manual Curation:** Tree is 100% derived from existing artifact content. Adding a Prior-Work reference = adding to tree.
126: 
127: ### 3. Interaction: Expand/Collapse, Filtering, Verification State?
128: 
129: **Answer:** Rich interaction model optimized for triage and orientation.
130: 
131: **Core Interactions:**
132: 
133: | Feature | Implementation | Use Case |
134: |---------|----------------|----------|
135: | **Expand/Collapse** | Click node to toggle children | Navigate large trees |
136: | **Filter by Type** | Checkboxes: investigations, decisions, models, issues | Focus on specific artifact types |
137: | **Filter by Area** | Dropdown: spawn, completion, dashboard, etc. | Focus on specific domains |
138: | **Search** | Fuzzy search on titles | Find specific artifacts |
139: | **Highlight Verification State** | Color coding: ◉ (complete), ◇ (triage), ● (in progress) | See work status at a glance |
140: | **Show Health Smells** | Warnings icon on clusters with 15+ investigations but no decision | Spot synthesis needs |
141: | **Click to Navigate** | Click node → navigate to file or beads issue | Quick access to artifacts |
142: 
143: **Visual Design:**
144: 
145: ```
146: ◉ Investigation (complete)     - Green
147: ◇ Investigation (triage)       - Yellow  
148: ● Investigation (in progress)  - Blue
149: ★ Decision                     - Gold
150: ◆ Model                        - Purple
151: ◈ Guide                        - Cyan
152: ◐ Issue (open)                 - Orange
153: ◓ Issue (closed)               - Gray
154: ⚠ Cluster smell (15+ invs)    - Red badge
155: ```
156: 
157: **Interaction Modes:**
158: 
159: 1. **Browse Mode (default):** Full tree, all types visible
160: 2. **Triage Mode:** Filter to show only clusters with health smells (many investigations, no decisions)
161: 3. **Verification Mode:** Show only recently added nodes (last 7 days) with status highlighting
162: 
163: **Keyboard Navigation:**
164: - `j/k` - Navigate up/down  
165: - `Enter` - Expand/collapse
166: - `o` - Open artifact in editor
167: - `f` - Focus search
168: - `/` - Quick filter
169: 
170: ### 4. Integration with Verify Flow
171: 
172: **Answer:** Show tree when daemon pauses, highlight new deliverable in context.
173: 
174: **Verification Context Protocol:**
175: 
176: ```bash
177: # During verification (orch complete flow)
178: 1. Agent completes work, reports Phase: Complete
179: 2. Daemon detects completion, pauses before next spawn
180: 3. Orchestrator runs: orch complete orch-go-xxxx
181: 4. Before explain-back prompt, show:
182:    
183:    📍 Where this deliverable fits:
184:    
185:    orch-go knowledge tree (filtered to current cluster)
186:    │
187:    ├─◉ Entropy Spiral
188:    │ ├─◉ investigations/2026-02-14-inv-entropy-spiral-deep-analysis.md
189:    │ │ ├─★ decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md  ← existing
190:    │ │ │ └─● orch-go-7jl  Daemon pause after N completions [NEW]  ← deliverable fits here
191:    
192:    This shows Dylan:
193:    - What cluster the work belongs to
194:    - What parent artifacts it builds on  
195:    - Where it fits in the lineage
196:    
197: 5. Dylan proceeds with explain-back with full context
198: ```
199: 
200: **Implementation:**
201: 
202: ```go
203: func ShowVerificationContext(beadsID string) error {
204:     // 1. Extract deliverable type and lineage from SYNTHESIS.md or beads description
205:     deliverable := extractDeliverable(beadsID)
206:     
207:     // 2. Find parent artifacts (what investigation/decision spawned this?)
208:     parents := findParentArtifacts(deliverable)
209:     
210:     // 3. Render tree filtered to relevant cluster, highlight new node
211:     tree := extractKnowledgeTree(".")
212:     cluster := findCluster(tree, parents)
213:     
214:     // 4. Render ASCII tree with [NEW] marker
215:     renderTree(cluster, highlightNode(beadsID))
216:     
217:     return nil
218: }
219: ```
220: 
221: **Benefits:**
222: - Dylan sees context before explain-back
223: - Reduces "where does this fit?" questions
224: - Makes verification more informed
225: - Natural checkpoint for understanding before approval
226: 
227: ### 5. CLI vs Web: Should 'orch tree' or 'kb tree' Exist?
228: 
229: **Answer:** Both. Different use cases.
230: 
231: **CLI Command Design:**
232: 
233: ```bash
234: # Quick lookup - show full tree
235: orch tree
236: 
237: # Filter to cluster
238: orch tree --cluster "entropy-spiral"
239: 
240: # Show only specific depth
241: orch tree --depth 2
242: 
243: # JSON output for scripting
244: orch tree --format json
245: 
246: # Show only artifacts with health smells
247: orch tree --smells
248: 
249: # Show what's new in last N days
250: orch tree --since 7d
251: ```
252: 
253: **Ownership:**
254: - `orch tree` - Lives in orch-go, focuses on orchestration artifacts (workspaces, synthesis, handoffs)
255: - `kb tree` - Lives in kb-cli, focuses on knowledge artifacts (investigations, decisions, models)
256: - Overlap is fine - both can render full tree from different perspectives
257: 
258: **Web Dashboard:**
259: - Full-featured browser with interaction, state persistence
260: - Primary interface for exploration and triage
261: - Supports all use cases CLI doesn't (expand/collapse, filtering, visual design)
262: 
263: **Decision Matrix:**
264: 
265: | Use Case | Tool |
266: |----------|------|
267: | Quick orientation at session start | CLI: `orch tree` |
268: | Deep triage for synthesis needs | Web: `/knowledge-tree` |
269: | Scripting/automation | CLI: `orch tree --format json` |
270: | Verification context | CLI: inline during `orch complete` |
271: | Browsing knowledge landscape | Web: `/knowledge-tree` |
272: 
273: **Recommendation:** Implement CLI first (simpler, faster MVP), add web dashboard second (richer interaction).
274: 
275: ### 6. Grouping: How to Determine Top-Level Clusters?
276: 
277: **Answer:** Multi-signal clustering with fallback hierarchy.
278: 
279: **Clustering Strategy:**
280: 
281: ```
282: Priority 1: Explicit area: labels (if present in beads integration)
283: Priority 2: Filesystem paths (investigations/synthesized/{cluster}/)  
284: Priority 3: Shared Prior-Work references (investigations citing same root)
285: Priority 4: Lexical similarity (embedding-based clustering as fallback)
286: ```
287: 
288: **Examples:**
289: 
290: | Cluster Name | Signal | Evidence |
291: |--------------|--------|----------|
292: | Entropy Spiral | Filesystem | `.kb/investigations/synthesized/entropy-spiral/*.md` |
293: | Serve Performance | Filesystem | `.kb/investigations/synthesized/serve-performance/*.md` |
294: | Coaching Plugin | Filesystem | `.kb/investigations/synthesized/coaching-plugin/*.md` |
295: | Completion Verification | Model | `.kb/models/completion-verification/` + related investigations |
296: | Daemon Autonomous Operation | Model | `.kb/models/daemon-autonomous-operation/` + related investigations |
297: 
298: **Cluster Inference Algorithm:**
299: 
300: ```go
301: func inferTopLevelClusters(graph *KnowledgeGraph) []Cluster {
302:     clusters := []Cluster{}
303:     
304:     // 1. Filesystem-based clusters (investigations/synthesized/{name}/)
305:     fsClusters := extractFilesystemClusters(".kb/investigations/synthesized")
306:     clusters = append(clusters, fsClusters...)
307:     
308:     // 2. Model-based clusters (.kb/models/{name}/ + referencing investigations)
309:     modelClusters := extractModelClusters(".kb/models", graph)
310:     clusters = append(clusters, modelClusters...)
311:     
312:     // 3. Decision-based clusters (decisions + citing investigations + spawned issues)
313:     decisionClusters := extractDecisionClusters(".kb/decisions", graph)
314:     clusters = append(clusters, decisionClusters...)
315:     
316:     // 4. Orphan investigations (no clear cluster) → "Uncategorized"
317:     orphans := findOrphans(graph, clusters)
318:     if len(orphans) > 0 {
319:         clusters = append(clusters, Cluster{Name: "Uncategorized", Nodes: orphans})
320:     }
321:     
322:     return clusters
323: }
324: ```
325: 
326: **Cluster Display:**
327: 
328: ```
329: orch-go knowledge tree
330: │
331: ├─◉ Entropy Spiral (4 investigations, 2 decisions, 5 issues)  ⚠ health smell
332: ├─◉ Serve Performance (7 investigations, 1 decision, 3 issues)
333: ├─◆ Completion Verification (31 investigations, 1 model, 3 probes)
334: ├─◆ Daemon Autonomous Operation (12 investigations, 1 model, 2 probes)
335: ├─◉ Coaching Plugin (2 investigations, 1 decision)
336: └─◉ Uncategorized (23 investigations)

338: 
339: **Health Smells:**
340: - ⚠ 15+ investigations without a decision or model = needs synthesis
341: - ⚠ Decision without any spawned issues = not acted on
342: - ⚠ Model without probes = untested model claims
343: 
344: ## Use Case Walkthrough
345: 
346: ### Use Case 1: Session Start Orientation
347: 
348: **Scenario:** Dylan starts a new orchestrator session, needs to see the knowledge landscape.
349: 
350: **Flow:**
351: 1. Dylan runs: `orch tree --smells`
352: 2. CLI renders tree highlighting clusters with health smells
353: 3. Dylan sees: "Entropy Spiral has 4 investigations and 2 decisions with 5 spawned issues"
354: 4. Dylan decides: "This cluster is active, I should review before starting new work"
355: 5. Dylan opens dashboard: `/knowledge-tree?cluster=entropy-spiral`
356: 6. Dashboard shows full lineage, Dylan understands current state
357: 
358: **Value:** Immediate orientation without reading hundreds of files.
359: 
360: ### Use Case 2: Triage for Synthesis Needs
361: 
362: **Scenario:** Orchestrator needs to find investigation clusters that need synthesis into decisions or models.
363: 
364: **Flow:**
365: 1. Orchestrator reviews: `/knowledge-tree` in Triage Mode (filter: health smells)
366: 2. Dashboard highlights: "23 investigations in Uncategorized, no decision/model"
367: 3. Orchestrator spawns: `orch spawn investigation "synthesize uncategorized investigations"`
368: 4. Investigation reviews cluster, proposes decision or model
369: 5. After synthesis, health smell disappears from tree
370: 
371: **Value:** Systematic triage without manual file scanning.
372: 
373: ### Use Case 3: Verification Context
374: 
375: **Scenario:** Agent completes work, Dylan needs to verify before next spawn.
376: 
377: **Flow:**
378: 1. Agent reports: `Phase: Complete` for orch-go-7jl
379: 2. Daemon pauses, Dylan runs: `orch complete orch-go-7jl`
380: 3. Before explain-back, CLI shows tree snippet:
381:    ```
382:    📍 Where this deliverable fits:
383:    ├─◉ Entropy Spiral
384:    │ ├─★ decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md
385:    │ │ └─● orch-go-7jl  Daemon pause after N completions [NEW]
386:    ```
387: 4. Dylan sees this implements the decision about verification constraints
388: 5. Dylan proceeds with informed explain-back
389: 
390: **Value:** Context before verification reduces cognitive load.
391: 
392: ### Use Case 4: New Session Claude Orientation
393: 
394: **Scenario:** New Claude spawned, needs to understand project structure quickly.
395: 
396: **Flow:**
397: 1. SPAWN_CONTEXT includes: "Run `orch tree --depth 2` for project orientation"
398: 2. Agent runs command, sees top-level clusters and key artifacts
399: 3. Agent understands: "Main work areas are Entropy Spiral, Serve Performance, Completion Verification"
400: 4. Agent proceeds with context-aware work
401: 
402: **Value:** Faster onboarding for spawned agents.

404: ## Implementation Phases

437: ## Technical Decisions
438: 
439: ### Parser Implementation
440: 
441: **Option A: Regex-based markdown parsing**
442: - Pros: Simple, no dependencies, fast
443: - Cons: Brittle, doesn't handle nested structures well
444: 
445: **Option B: Markdown AST parser (goldmark)**
446: - Pros: Robust, handles all markdown features, extensible
447: - Cons: Heavier dependency, more complex
448: 
449: **Recommendation:** Start with regex for Prior-Work tables (simple, well-structured), use goldmark if complexity grows.
450: 
451: ### Tree Rendering
452: 
453: **Option A: Custom tree renderer**
454: - Pros: Full control, custom features
455: - Cons: Reinventing the wheel
456: 
457: **Option B: Existing library (go-tree-printer)**
458: - Pros: Battle-tested, saves time
459: - Cons: May not support all features we need
460: 
461: **Recommendation:** Custom renderer (it's ~100 lines for ASCII tree, gives us exact format from example).
462: 
463: ### Cluster Detection
464: 
465: **Option A: Filesystem-only**
466: - Pros: Simple, explicit, no magic
467: - Cons: Requires manual directory organization
468: 
469: **Option B: Embedding-based clustering**
470: - Pros: Automatic, discovers hidden patterns
471: - Cons: Opaque, requires ML dependencies
472: 
473: **Recommendation:** Filesystem-first (explicit clusters exist), add embedding-based for orphans later if needed.
474: 
475: ## Constraints & Validation
476: 
477: ### Constraint 1: Work with Existing .kb/ Structure ✓
478: - **Validation:** No new metadata required, all relationships extracted from existing Prior-Work tables, Evidence sections, filesystem hierarchy

488: - **Test:** Dylan reviews tree instead of manually scanning .kb/ directory

490: ## Open Questions
491: 
492: 1. **Should tree show temporal ordering?** (newest first vs alphabetical)
493:    - Recommendation: Newest first for investigations, alphabetical for decisions/models
494:    
495: 2. **How deep should default expansion go?** (1 level vs 2 vs fully expanded)
496:    - Recommendation: 2 levels (shows clusters and top-level artifacts)
497:    
498: 3. **Should we cache the extracted tree?** (recompute on every render vs cache with invalidation)
499:    - Recommendation: Cache with invalidation on .kb/ file changes (performance optimization)
500:    
501: 4. **Should beads issues be included in tree or separate view?** (integrated vs split)
502:    - Recommendation: Integrated (issues are outcomes of decisions, natural tree extension)

521: ## Design Addendum: Two-View Model (from Dylan review, Feb 14)
522: 
523: ### The Issue Role Question
524: 
525: Dylan asked: "Are issues secondary or on the same level as knowledge artifacts?"
526: 
527: **Answer:** Issues are the **active manifestation** of knowledge artifacts — the live edge of work. They're not leaf nodes hanging off decisions. They're where the energy is.
528: 
529: This leads to a **two-view model** — same data, same tree, different root:
530: 
531: ### Knowledge View (`orch tree`)
532: 
533: Knowledge artifacts are primary. Issues hang off as "what this knowledge produced."
534: Question it answers: **"What do we understand?"**
535: 
536: ```
537: orch-go knowledge tree
538: │
539: ├─◉ Entropy Spiral
540: │ ├─◉ post-mortems/2026-01-02-system-spiral-dec27-jan02.md
541: │ ├─◉ investigations/2026-02-14-inv-entropy-spiral-deep-analysis.md
542: │ │ ├─★ decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md
543: │ │ │ ├─● orch-go-5sc  Decision record                    CLOSED
544: │ │ │ ├─● orch-go-7jl  Daemon pause after N completions   CLOSED
545: │ │ │ └─◇ orch-go-6th  Skill system update                triage:review
546: │ │ └─● orch-go-agr  Trajectory audit                     IN PROGRESS
547: │ └─◉ handoffs/2026-02-13-entropy-spiral-recovery.md
548: ```
549: 
550: ### Work View (`orch tree --work`)
551: 
552: Issues are primary. Knowledge artifacts hang off as "where this work came from."
553: Question it answers: **"What are we doing and why?"**
554: 
555: This is likely the view Dylan opens most often — when the daemon has paused and he sits down to review.
556: 
557: ```
558: orch-go work tree
559: │
560: ├─⚡ NEEDS VERIFICATION (3)
561: │ ├─● orch-go-7jl  Daemon pause after N completions
562: │ │ └─ from ★ verifiability-first-hard-constraint
563: │ │    └─ from ◉ entropy-spiral-deep-analysis
564: │ ├─● orch-go-tyi  Explain-back verification gate
565: │ │ └─ from ★ verifiability-first-hard-constraint
566: │ └─● orch-go-8f7  Knowledge tree visualization
567: │   └─ from design session (Feb 14)
568: │
569: ├─◇ TRIAGE:REVIEW (2)
570: │ ├─◇ orch-go-6th  Skill system update
571: │ │ └─ from ★ verifiability-first-hard-constraint
572: │ └─◇ orch-go-cem  Click freeze reactive capture
573: │
574: ├─● IN PROGRESS (1)
575: │ └─● orch-go-agr  Trajectory audit
576: │   └─ from ◉ entropy-spiral-deep-analysis
577: │
578: └─░ QUEUED (7)
579:   ├─● orch-go-2qj  Model staleness detection
580:   ├─● orch-go-4tz  Completion accretion gate
581:   └─ ... 5 more
582: ```
583: 
584: ### Why Two Views
585: 
586: | View | When | Question | Primary nodes | Secondary nodes |
587: |------|------|----------|---------------|-----------------|
588: | Knowledge | Orientation, triage, synthesis | "What do we understand?" | Investigations, decisions, models | Issues as outcomes |
589: | Work | Review session, daemon pause, daily standup | "What are we doing and why?" | Issues grouped by state | Knowledge artifacts as provenance |
590: 
591: Same underlying data graph. Same extraction algorithm. Different traversal root and grouping logic.
592: 
593: ### Impact on Implementation Phases
594: 
595: Phase 1 (CLI MVP) should include **both** views:
596: - `orch tree` — knowledge view (default)
597: - `orch tree --work` — work view (likely used more often)
598: 
599: The work view is cheap once the knowledge view exists — it's the same tree traversed from the other end.
600: 
601: ### Dylan's Note
602: 
603: "This UI design could entirely transform how I work if we do this right."
604: 
605: The key is: **Dylan drives what 'right' means through usage.** CLI MVP ships, Dylan uses it, we iterate. This is the verifiability-first loop applied to its own tooling.

613: 4. Test with current .kb/ structure

126: 	// If no root nodes found, return all nodes
127: 	if len(rootNodes) == 0 {
128: 		return cluster.Nodes
129: 	}
130: 
131: 	return rootNodes

134: // BuildWorkTree builds the work view tree (issues as primary nodes)
135: func BuildWorkTree(kbDir string, projectDir string, opts TreeOptions) ([]*KnowledgeNode, error) {
136: 	// Initialize beads client (CLI client for simplicity)
137: 	var client beads.BeadsClient
138: 	client = beads.NewCLIClient()
139: 
140: 	// Get all issues
141: 	issues, err := client.List(&beads.ListArgs{})
142: 	if err != nil {
143: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("failed to list issues: %w", err)
144: 	}
145: 
146: 	// Parse all knowledge artifacts to build a reference map
147: 	// This allows us to find which artifacts issues reference
148: 	artifactMap := make(map[string]*KnowledgeNode)
149: 
150: 	// Parse investigations
151: 	invDir := filepath.Join(kbDir, "investigations")
152: 	if invNodes, _, err := ParseInvestigations(invDir); err == nil {
153: 		for _, node := range invNodes {
154: 			// Index by filename
155: 			filename := filepath.Base(node.Path)
156: 			artifactMap[filename] = node
157: 			artifactMap[node.Path] = node
158: 		}
159: 	}
160: 
161: 	// Parse decisions
162: 	decDir := filepath.Join(kbDir, "decisions")
163: 	if decNodes, _, err := ParseDecisions(decDir); err == nil {
164: 		for _, node := range decNodes {
165: 			filename := filepath.Base(node.Path)
166: 			artifactMap[filename] = node
167: 			artifactMap[node.Path] = node
168: 		}
169: 	}
170: 
171: 	// Parse models
172: 	modelDir := filepath.Join(kbDir, "models")
173: 	if modelNodes, _, err := ParseModels(modelDir); err == nil {
174: 		for _, node := range modelNodes {
175: 			artifactMap[node.ID] = node
176: 			artifactMap[node.Path] = node
177: 		}
178: 	}
179: 
180: 	// Convert beads issues to KnowledgeNodes and link to artifacts
181: 	var issueNodes []*KnowledgeNode
182: 	for _, issue := range issues {
183: 		issueNode := convertIssueToNode(&issue)
184: 
185: 		// Parse issue description to find references to knowledge artifacts
186: 		// Look for file paths in description
187: 		linkedArtifacts := findReferencedArtifacts(issue.Description, artifactMap)
188: 		issueNode.Children = linkedArtifacts
189: 
190: 		issueNodes = append(issueNodes, issueNode)
191: 	}
192: 
193: 	return issueNodes, nil
194: }

196: // convertIssueToNode converts a beads Issue to a KnowledgeNode
197: func convertIssueToNode(issue *beads.Issue) *KnowledgeNode {
198: 	status := StatusOpen
199: 	switch issue.Status {
200: 	case "closed":
201: 		status = StatusClosed
202: 	case "in_progress":
203: 		status = StatusInProgress
204: 	case "open":
205: 		if hasLabel(issue.Labels, "triage:review") {
206: 			status = StatusTriage
207: 		} else {
208: 			status = StatusOpen
209: 		}
210: 	}
211: 
212: 	return &KnowledgeNode{
213: 		ID:       issue.ID,
214: 		Type:     NodeTypeIssue,
215: 		Title:    issue.Title,
216: 		Path:     issue.ID, // Use ID as path for issues
217: 		Status:   status,
218: 		Children: []*KnowledgeNode{},
219: 		Metadata: map[string]interface{}{
220: 			"priority": issue.Priority,
221: 			"type":     issue.IssueType,
222: 		},
223: 	}
224: }
225: 
226: // hasLabel checks if a label exists in the labels slice
227: func hasLabel(labels []string, target string) bool {
228: 	for _, label := range labels {
229: 		if label == target {
230: 			return true
231: 		}
232: 	}
233: 	return false
234: }

236: // findReferencedArtifacts finds knowledge artifacts referenced in text
237: func findReferencedArtifacts(text string, artifactMap map[string]*KnowledgeNode) []*KnowledgeNode {
238: 	var artifacts []*KnowledgeNode
239: 	seen := make(map[string]bool)
240: 
241: 	// Look for .kb/ paths in the text
242: 	// Match patterns like: .kb/investigations/2026-02-14-inv-something.md
243: 	// or decisions/2026-02-14-something.md
244: 	pathRegex := regexp.MustCompile(`(?:\.kb/)?(?:investigations|decisions|models)/[^\s\)]+\.md`)
245: 	matches := pathRegex.FindAllString(text, -1)
246: 
247: 	for _, match := range matches {
248: 		// Normalize the path
249: 		normalized := match
250: 		if !strings.HasPrefix(normalized, ".kb/") {
251: 			normalized = ".kb/" + normalized
252: 		}
253: 
254: 		// Try to find the artifact
255: 		if artifact, ok := artifactMap[normalized]; ok && !seen[artifact.ID] {
256: 			artifacts = append(artifacts, artifact)
257: 			seen[artifact.ID] = true
258: 		} else {
259: 			// Try just the filename
260: 			filename := filepath.Base(match)
261: 			if artifact, ok := artifactMap[filename]; ok && !seen[artifact.ID] {
262: 				artifacts = append(artifacts, artifact)
263: 				seen[artifact.ID] = true
264: 			}
265: 		}
266: 	}
267: 
268: 	return artifacts
269: }

88: // BuildRelationshipGraph builds a graph of nodes connected by relationships
89: func BuildRelationshipGraph(nodes []*KnowledgeNode, relationships []Relationship) map[string][]*KnowledgeNode {
90: 	graph := make(map[string][]*KnowledgeNode)
91: 	nodeMap := make(map[string]*KnowledgeNode)
92: 
93: 	// Index nodes by their path AND by their ID (for models, ID is directory, Path is .md file)
94: 	for _, node := range nodes {
95: 		nodeMap[node.Path] = node
96: 		if node.ID != node.Path {
97: 			nodeMap[node.ID] = node
98: 		}
99: 	}
100: 
101: 	// Build parent-child relationships
102: 	for _, rel := range relationships {
103: 		parent := findNodeByPath(nodeMap, rel.From)
104: 		child := findNodeByPath(nodeMap, rel.To)
105: 
106: 		if parent != nil && child != nil {
107: 			// Add child to parent's children if not already there
108: 			found := false
109: 			for _, existingChild := range parent.Children {
110: 				if existingChild.ID == child.ID {
111: 					found = true
112: 					break
113: 				}
114: 			}
115: 			if !found {
116: 				parent.Children = append(parent.Children, child)
117: 			}
118: 
119: 			// Track in graph
120: 			graph[parent.ID] = append(graph[parent.ID], child)
121: 		}
122: 	}
123: 
124: 	return graph
125: }
126: 
127: // findNodeByPath finds a node by path, handling both exact matches and directory matches
128: func findNodeByPath(nodeMap map[string]*KnowledgeNode, path string) *KnowledgeNode {
129: 	// Try exact match first
130: 	if node, ok := nodeMap[path]; ok {
131: 		return node
132: 	}
133: 
134: 	// For directory paths (like .kb/models/completion-verification/), try to match by ID
135: 	// Models use directory as ID, so check if any node's ID matches this path
136: 	for _, node := range nodeMap {
137: 		if node.ID == path {
138: 			return node
139: 		}
140: 		// Also try matching directory to directory
141: 		if strings.HasSuffix(path, "/") && node.Type == NodeTypeModel {
142: 			if strings.HasSuffix(node.ID, "/") && node.ID == path {
143: 				return node
144: 			}
145: 			// Try without trailing slash
146: 			if node.ID == strings.TrimSuffix(path, "/") {
147: 				return node
148: 			}
149: 		}
150: 	}
151: 
152: 	return nil
153: }

Good. Now let me analyze the existing code more carefully to understand the issues:

Looking at `parseInvestigation` in parser.go:141-148:
```go
// Prior-Work table lists SOURCES (parents), so reverse the direction:
// The target is the parent, this investigation is the child
relationships = append(relationships, Relationship{
    From:         absTargetPath, // Parent (source)
    To:           path,          // Child (this investigation)
    RelationType: relType,
    Verified:     strings.ToLower(verified) == "yes",
})
```

This creates relationships where `From` is the parent and `To` is the child. This seems correct.

But in `parseDecision` lines 230-242:
```go
// Decision references investigation, so decision is parent, investigation is child
relationships = append(relationships, Relationship{
    From:         path,          // Decision (parent)
    To:           absTargetPath, // Investigation (child)
    RelationType: "references",
    Verified:     true,
})
```

Wait, this is WRONG. If a decision references an investigation in its Evidence section, that means the investigation is the PARENT (the evidence that led to the decision), not the child. The decision should be nested UNDER the investigation, not above it.

So the key issues are:
1. Decision→Investigation relationship is backwards (should be Investigation→Decision)
2. Synthesized From in models doesn't create relationships at all
3. buildClusterTree doesn't properly use the relationship graph to nest children
4. No cycle detection

245: 	matches := pathRegex.FindAllString(text, -1)
246: 
247: 	for _, match := range matches {

249: 		normalized := match
250: 		if !strings.HasPrefix(normalized, ".kb/") {
251: 			normalized = ".kb/" + normalized
252: 		}
253: 
254: 		// Try to find the artifact
255: 		if artifact, ok := artifactMap[normalized]; ok && !seen[artifact.ID] {
256: 			artifacts = append(artifacts, artifact)
257: 			seen[artifact.ID] = true
258: 		} else {
259: 			// Try just the filename
260: 			filename := filepath.Base(match)
261: 			if artifact, ok := artifactMap[filename]; ok && !seen[artifact.ID] {
262: 				artifacts = append(artifacts, artifact)
263: 				seen[artifact.ID] = true
264: 			}
265: 		}
266: 	}

Wait, let me re-examine buildClusterTree. I added cycle-safe cloning that filters children to only those in the cluster. But if decisions are in a different cluster ("decisions"), they won't be in the clusterNodes map for the "Entropy Spiral" cluster, so they'll be filtered out!

This is the bug! The buildClusterTree function's cloneNodeRecursive filters children to only those "in the cluster", but decisions/models/issues are in different clusters, so they get filtered out.

The fix is to NOT filter children by cluster membership. Instead, buildClusterTree should include all children regardless of which cluster they're assigned to. The cluster assignment is just for finding root nodes, not for filtering the tree.


The solution is to remove the cluster membership check from cloneNodeRecursive and include all children in the tree structure, since the cluster assignment only determines which nodes appear as roots.

part.text: `filepath.Walk` provides the `path` variable which will be a relative path if `dir` is relative (which it is - `.kb/decisions`). So the file paths being passed to `parseDecision` are relative paths like `.kb/decisions/2026-02-13-lifecycle-ownership-boundaries.md`.Then when `resolveKbPath` tries to split by `/.kb/`, it splits into:- `[".kb/decisions/2026-02-13-lifecycle-ownership-boundaries.md"]` (only one part, no split)So `len(parts) > 1` is false, and it falls through to the last case which does `filepath.Join(dir, referencePath)` where `dir` is `.kb/decisions` and `referencePath` is `.kb/investigations/...`.The fix is to make sure file paths are absolute before processing, or to fix `resolveKbPath` to handle relative paths correctly.
